item.title: Iran war has exposed the weakness of the dollar
item.description: As Opec members have long understood, it is not a good idea to give users of your product an incentive to find alternatives

item.title: Is the BoJ ready to take Japan back to reality?
item.description: This month’s probable rate increase is in keeping with the moment

item.title: The case for trade, remade
item.description: There are good reasons to believe it is likely to follow the rewiring of global finance earlier this century

item.title: Wirecard trial shows the risks of Germany’s slow legal process
item.description: Financial crime cases can be vulnerable to long delays and procedural gridlock

item.title: Oh! What a weird war for investors
item.description: Buying oil might be the right move now

item.title: Nike’s comeback may not come all the way back
item.description: Rehashing old hits and leaving shop shelves empty has enabled upstarts to steal a march

item.title: An unstable truce in the Middle East
item.description: Iran has been severely degraded but for Trump there are lessons to learn

item.title: The UK needs to make choices on tech strategy
item.description: As with Brexit, public anger could swing behind the idea that we need to take back control

item.title: Why the ‘SaaSpocalypse’ doomsayers are wrong
item.description: Every major tech platform shift follows the same pattern: short-term pain for long-term gain

item.title: How this oil shock unwinds
item.description: At a rate of 1mn barrels a day of overproduction, it would take not far off two years to make up the buffers

item.title: The madness of the Madman Theory
item.description: An idea that didn’t work for Richard Nixon probably won’t for Donald Trump

item.title: How I became a meme in Nigeria
item.description: My moment of internet fame reflects the country’s sceptical political mood

item.title: Toyota keeps hydrogen as a hedge
item.description: Retaining flexibility may prove more valuable than going all in too early on a single bet

item.title: Armageddon is off . . . for now
item.description: Both the US and Iran can claim victory of a sort but many questions remain unresolved

item.title: Earnings expectations are too high
item.description: Plus AI and jobs

item.title: When persistence prevails
item.description: After 593 monthly editions, a legend of role-playing games magazines has finally called it a day

item.title: Freedom itself is at stake in Hungary
item.description: Sunday’s election will show if it’s possible to defeat Orbán’s illiberal democracy

item.title: How can we protect my mother’s estate after she passes away?
item.description: Her new partner lives with her and she wants to grant him the right to remain living in the property

item.title: The dystopian fantasy of uselessness
item.description: Fears that AI will strip our lives of meaning are based on three fallacies

item.title: China’s new trade front with the world — services
item.description: The country is rapidly moving up the global ranks as an exporter of knowledge-intensive products

item.title: Musk struggles to crack the Indian market
item.description: More diplomacy by entrepreneur might be needed amid regulatory challenges and domestic competition to his businesses

item.title: Bill Ackman strikes a chord with Universal bid
item.description: But financier needs the backing of Vincent Bolloré and his Vivendi media group

item.title: Sudan’s grassroots groups — a model in the midst of war
item.description: The Emergency Response Rooms are a template for aid delivery elsewhere

item.title: Oil shock sorts cruise lines into the hedged and hedged nots
item.description: Carnival is paying price for not locking in its fuel exposure when compared to competitors

item.title: Why an airport water-art display may spark US airline consolidation
item.description: JetBlue’s viability as standalone entity hinges on plethora of factors it has little control over
